data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Disney (DIS) After Recent Streaming And Parks Updates?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Walt Disney at around US$94.32 is a bargain or a value trap, you are not alone. That is exactly what this article will help you unpack.
  • The stock has seen a 3.7% decline over the last 7 days, an 11.1% decline over 30 days, a 15.7% decline year to date, and a 3.4% decline over the past year. This naturally raises questions about potential opportunity or shifting risk perceptions.
  • Recent headlines have focused on Walt Disney's content pipeline, theme park operations, and ongoing moves in streaming. Investors now have fresh information to weigh against the share price. These updates provide important context for the recent pullbacks and how the market is reacting to the company’s evolving business mix.
  • With a valuation score of 5 out of 6 checks for being undervalued, the company looks interesting on traditional metrics. The next sections will walk through the main valuation approaches, followed by a more complete way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Find out why Walt Disney's -3.4% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Walt Disney Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value using an appropriate rate. It focuses on cash that could theoretically be returned to shareholders over time.

For Walt Disney, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $8.6b. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analysts and extrapolated estimates point to projected Free Cash Flow of about $14.1b in 2030, with a series of annual forecasts between 2026 and 2035 supplied by analysts and Simply Wall St’s own extrapolations.

Pulling these projections together and discounting them back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of around $98.79 per share. Compared with the current share price of about $94.32, the DCF suggests the stock trades at roughly a 4.5% discount. This indicates a mild gap rather than a huge disconnect.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Walt Disney is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Walt Disney.

Approach 2: Walt Disney Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars of price the market is asking for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually means a lower one.

Walt Disney currently trades on a P/E of about 13.64x. That sits below the Entertainment industry average of about 35.11x and well below the peer group average of roughly 61.15x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework, which estimates what a more tailored P/E might look like after considering earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and risks, points to a Fair Ratio of about 24.91x.

This Fair Ratio is more specific to Walt Disney than simple peer or industry comparisons because it adjusts for company level characteristics rather than assuming one size fits all. Comparing the current P/E of 13.64x with the Fair Ratio of 24.91x indicates that the shares appear undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Narratives bring that idea to life by letting you attach a clear story to your numbers, linking your view of Walt Disney’s future revenue, earnings and margins to a forecast, and then to a fair value that you can compare with today’s US$94.32 share price.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives are an accessible tool used by millions of investors. You can see and build different stories for the same company, each with its own set of assumptions, cash flow or earnings estimates, and an implied fair value that updates automatically when new earnings, news or guidance is released.

For Walt Disney, one Narrative might focus on parks, experiences and AI partnerships, using assumptions like a fair value of US$134.63 or US$131.56 per share and specific revenue growth and discount rates. Another Narrative might focus on streaming competition and higher production costs, with a fair value around US$95.94. What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects expectations for future growth and the level of risk investors see in the business. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple.

Approach 1: Airbus Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today to get a present value per share.

For Airbus, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about €4.23b. Across the 10 year span from 2026 to 2035, the model uses a series of forecast free cash flows in the range of roughly €4.44b to €14.12b, each discounted back to reflect the time value of money. Adding these discounted values together and adjusting for the share count produces an estimated intrinsic value of about €329.18 per share.

Compared with the recent share price of €159.18, this model output indicates the stock trades at a 51.6% discount to the DCF estimate, which suggests the shares may be significantly undervalued on this basis alone.

data.title: Fermi’s Project Matador Buildout Faces Tenant Gap And Revenue Uncertainty
data.description:
• Fermi has yet to secure a cornerstone tenant for its flagship Project Matador campus, despite progressing permits that target 17 GW of power capacity.
  • The company has made substantial capital commitments and lined up equipment financing, but no anchor customer contract has been confirmed.
  • This gap between infrastructure build out and tenant sign on is raising questions about future revenue visibility and the private grid model.

For investors watching NasdaqGS:FRMI, the lack of an anchor tenant at Project Matador now sits alongside a share price of $5.36 and sharp recent share price moves, including a 25.9% decline over the past week and a 46.2% decline over the past month. Year to date, the stock is down 39.2%. This indicates that the market is already reacting to execution risk around Fermi's large scale plans.

The core issue now is whether Fermi can turn its regulatory and financing groundwork into contracted demand at Project Matador. Until a cornerstone tenant is confirmed, questions around utilization, cash generation and the durability of the private grid thesis are likely to remain central for anyone assessing the long term appeal of NasdaqGS:FRMI.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Fermi by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Fermi.

We've flagged 1 risk for Fermi. See which could impact your investment.

Fermi is pressing ahead with permits and equipment financing for Project Matador while still reporting a full year 2025 net loss of US$486.38 million and no signed cornerstone tenant. That combination puts execution squarely in focus. The company now has an approved 6 GW Clean Air Permit, another 5 GW application filed, and a US$165 million term loan for turbines on top of earlier warehouse facilities, all pointing to a capital intensive build before revenue visibility is clear. For readers, the key question is whether the private grid model tailored to hyperscalers will translate into long term contracts on acceptable terms, or whether the cost of carrying assets and high coupon debt will weigh on equity holders if tenant signings stay slow.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Large net loss of US$486.38 million in 2025, with the business still in a pre revenue phase and dependent on external financing.
  • ⚠️ Significant capital commitments into permits, turbines and private grid infrastructure without a cornerstone tenant increases the risk that assets are underutilised.
  • 🎁 Regulatory progress, including a 6 GW Clean Air Permit and an additional 5 GW application, gives Fermi a clearer path to build out up to 17 GW of capacity.
  • 🎁 Access to US$165 million of equipment financing and other warehouse facilities provides funding flexibility to secure key long lead time assets for Project Matador.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, the most important signals will be any announcement of a long term power or data center agreement at Project Matador, especially with large cloud or AI players that currently buy capacity from utilities and colocation providers. Investors may also want to track Fermi's cash runway, debt covenants under the Beal Credit Agreement, and progress on the Nuclear Regulatory Commission review. Together, those factors will influence how much room Fermi has to keep building while it works to convert interest from potential tenants into signed contracts.
